questions.q: At which point of $V(y^2 - x^3) \subset \mathbb{A}^2$ does the Jacobian criterion detect a singularity?
questions.explain: $f = y^2 - x^3$ has $\partial_x f = -3x^2$, $\partial_y f = 2y$. Both vanish only at the origin, where $f$ also vanishes — that is the cusp.

questions.q: For the nodal curve $y^2 = x^2 + x^3$ at the origin, what is the tangent cone?
choices: The pair of lines $y = x$ and $y = -x$
questions.explain: The lowest-degree term is $y^2 - x^2 = (y-x)(y+x)$, so the tangent cone is the union of the two lines $y = \pm x$.

questions.q: If $X \subset \mathbb{A}^n$ is cut out by $r$ equations and $\dim X = d$ at $p$, smoothness at $p$ means the Jacobian rank is:
questions.explain: Smoothness asks the codimension to be cut out transversally, so $\mathrm{rank}\,J(p) = n - d$. Equivalently, $\dim T_p X = d$.

questions.q: On the blow-up $\mathrm{Bl}_0 \mathbb{A}^2 \subset \mathbb{A}^2 \times \mathbb{P}^1$, the exceptional divisor over the origin is:
questions.explain: The fiber over $0$ is the projectivization of the tangent space — for $\mathbb{A}^2$ that is $\mathbb{P}^1$, parametrising lines through the origin.

questions.q: Which property characterises the blow-up $\mathrm{Bl}_Z X$ as a universal object?
choices: It is the unique smooth model of $X$
It is the smallest variety containing $Z$
It is universal among $X$-schemes on which the ideal sheaf $\mathcal{I}_Z$ pulls back to a locally principal (Cartier) ideal
questions.explain: The blow-up is defined by exactly this universal property: any map $f : Y \to X$ with $f^{-1}\mathcal{I}_Z$ invertible factors uniquely through $\mathrm{Bl}_Z X$.

questions.q: Outside the centre $Z$, the blow-up morphism $\pi : \mathrm{Bl}_Z X \to X$ is:
questions.explain: $\pi$ is birational and changes nothing away from $Z$. It is an isomorphism on the open complement $X \setminus Z$.

questions.q: Hironaka's theorem (1964) guarantees a proper birational $\pi : \tilde X \to X$ with $\tilde X$ smooth, under which assumption?
choices: $X$ is defined over a field of characteristic $0$
questions.explain: The classical statement is for varieties over a field of characteristic zero. Positive-characteristic resolution is open in dimension $\ge 4$.

questions.q: Where does Hironaka's resolution agree with $X$?
choices: Everywhere — it is an isomorphism globally
questions.explain: By construction $\pi$ is an iso over the smooth open subset; only the singular locus gets modified.

choices: a single blow-up at the singular locus
a finite sequence of blow-ups along smooth centres inside the singular locus
a quotient by a finite group
questions.explain: The argument tracks an invariant (multiplicity, Hilbert–Samuel function) that strictly drops after each blow-up at a carefully chosen smooth centre, terminating in a smooth model.

questions.q: Blowing up the cusp $y^2 = x^3$ at the origin yields a curve whose strict transform is:
questions.explain: Setting $y = tx$ gives $t^2 x^2 = x^3$, i.e. $x = t^2$ on the strict transform; then $y = t \cdot t^2 = t^3$. The single smooth point $t = 0$ replaces the cusp.

questions.q: The normalisation $\tilde C \to C$ of an irreducible nodal curve $C$ separates the node into:
choices: two distinct smooth points (one per branch)
questions.explain: Normalisation pulls apart the two analytic branches that the node identifies; each branch contributes one smooth preimage.

questions.q: Which constructions give the same resolution of a planar curve singularity?
choices: Blow-up of the singular point only
All three agree in dimension one
questions.explain: For curves, normalisation, iterated blow-ups, and Puiseux parametrisations all produce the same smooth model — the curve case is essentially settled.

questions.q: The dual graph of the minimal resolution of the $A_n$ surface singularity $x^2 + y^2 + z^{n+1} = 0$ is:
questions.explain: The exceptional divisor is a chain of $n$ smooth rational curves with intersection matrix $-\mathrm{Cartan}(A_n)$ — the $A_n$ Dynkin diagram.

questions.q: Brieskorn realised the ADE surface singularities as quotients $\mathbb{C}^2 / \Gamma$ where $\Gamma$ is:
questions.explain: Finite subgroups of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ are classified by ADE: cyclic ($A_n$), binary dihedral ($D_n$), and binary tetrahedral / octahedral / icosahedral ($E_6, E_7, E_8$).

choices: irreducible representations of $\Gamma$ (other than the trivial one) with exceptional curves of the resolution
characters of $\Gamma$ with cohomology classes of $X$
Sylow subgroups of $\Gamma$ with branch points
questions.explain: Non-trivial irreps of $\Gamma \subset \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ correspond bijectively to vertices of the Dynkin diagram, i.e. exceptional $\mathbb{P}^1$'s in the minimal resolution.

questions.q: In Mori's minimal model program, the role of resolution is to:
choices: produce the unique smooth projective model
give a smooth starting point from which to contract extremal rays toward a minimal model
replace a variety by its dual
questions.explain: MMP starts from a smooth (resolved) projective variety and runs a sequence of contractions and flips to reach a minimal or canonical model.

questions.q: A 'log resolution' of a pair $(X, D)$ produces a smooth $\tilde X$ on which:
choices: the total transform of $D$ together with the exceptional divisor is a simple normal crossing divisor
$\tilde X$ is itself singular but $D$ is smooth
questions.explain: Log resolution asks the preimage of $D$ plus exceptional divisors to have only transversal smooth components — the SNC condition powers motivic integration and weak factorisation.

questions.q: Which problem is still open as of today?
choices: Resolution of singularities in characteristic $p$ in dimension $\ge 4$
questions.explain: Hironaka settled char 0 in all dimensions; Abhyankar and Cossart–Piltant settled char $p$ up to dimension 3. Higher dimensions remain open; de Jong's alterations are the best general substitute.
